What Is the Dunning–Kruger Effect? Thought You Knew It, But Actually Didn't

The Dunning–Kruger effect is a cognitive bias where low-ability people overestimate themselves. The most famous part of the curve is "Mount Stupid" — early confidence spikes, real-world failure crashes it down, and only the people who push through that crash actually grow.
As a software engineer I read a lot, and one of the most well-known things I keep coming back to is the
. A lot of people don't really know what it is — or, more accurately, they think they know it, but they don't. So let's talk about it.
The Dunning–Kruger effect isn't just a theory — it's something that actually happens, to pretty much everyone. You, me, the people around us. Most of us just aren't aware it's happening.

It shows up most often in beginners, people changing careers, newbies in general. Why? Because Dunning–Kruger tends to hit hardest when you're learning a brand-new field with a huge body of knowledge. After picking up the basics, it's really easy to fall into the "
" illusion — and that's when you start to think you understand things you actually don't.

A real example from my own world: I work in education, so I see this all the time. A lot of people assume that finishing some course will land them a job, or that they're basically done after the course wraps up. Then they go interview and hit a wall.
Why does this happen? Is it the course or the teacher's fault?
Usually it's not on the teacher. It's mostly on the student.
When someone teaches a course, they teach based on the

— meaning they cover the 80% that actually matters in the industry, and the remaining 20% is something you have to figure out for yourself. That 20% often ends up being your real competitive edge later, but it's not stuff that comes up in everyday practice, so most teachers skip it. Honestly, if I were teaching I'd probably do the same — covering it all would take forever, and the difficulty would frustrate students.
Because when you're learning something new, two things keep you going:
. Those matter more than completeness, especially early on.
That said, if a student actually asks me, I'm happy to share. It's just that most beginners don't dig into that last 20%, because it takes too much time.

Part of this also comes down to modern life — a lot of people want "fast results" and overlook how much time and pain went into the people who actually became good at something. That's exactly where the first stage of Dunning–Kruger kicks in: the "
". You think you've figured it out, your confidence spikes, then reality starts pushing back. People start blaming external stuff and never admit they don't actually know — because, well, ego.
After Mount Stupid, things slowly start improving — but this part is brutal. You have to actually accept that
at this. Confidence tanks. A lot of people quit right here.

It really is hard. A lot of people drop off at Mount Stupid because they don't want to admit they're bad — so they bail. But honestly, this part is necessary. You have to feel the weight of being bad before you can actually grow.
It's not about thinking you're terrible forever — it's about understanding

Quick analogy: think back to when you first played a video game. You were probably bad at it. Random mobs and bosses kept killing you. But you didn't quit — you started looking up gear, stats, techniques. The growth process is the same. The only difference is in real life you don't get a "
Apply this to real life: keep learning, your knowledge keeps growing, and your confidence climbs alongside it. Eventually you reach the second stage — the "
" — and that's when real growth happens. Beginner becomes professional.

The path there is hard. Like I said, the climb to stage two is rough — some people quit, others stick with it. But here's the pattern I keep seeing: the people who stay humble, take feedback well, and grind through the hard parts are usually the ones who actually make it.
So set your target. Don't give up easily. Recognize where you suck. Take feedback seriously. Know yourself. That's how you stay off Mount Stupid and onto the real path toward mastery.
I'll close with my own story — I've spent a

of time learning and figuring things out to get where I am now. Everyone's brain is roughly the same; what differs is your background and environment, which means everyone grows at different speeds. But there are techniques you can pick up to grow faster.

The Dunning–Kruger effect is a cognitive bias where the less you know, the more confident you feel. This post walks through the four classic stages — Mount Stupid, the Valley of Despair, the Slope of Enlightenment and the road to mastery — and why beginners hit them so hard.
